sourceQuestion:
Where does the swap space reside?
A. RAM
B. Disk
C. ROM
D. On-chip cache

parsed.stem: Where does the swap space reside?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. Virtual memory implements the translation of a program's address space into physical memory address space.
B. Virtual memory allows each program to exceed the size of the primary memory.
C. Virtual memory increases the degree of multiprogramming.
D. Virtual memory reduces the context switching overhead.
sourceAnswer: D. Virtual memory reduces the context switching overhead.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is false?

options.text: Virtual memory implements the translation of a program's address space into physical memory address space.
Virtual memory allows each program to exceed the size of the primary memory.
Virtual memory increases the degree of multiprogramming.
Virtual memory reduces the context switching overhead.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a machine with 64 MB physical memory and a 32-bit virtual address space. If the page size is 4 KB, what is the approximate size of the page table?
A. 16 MB
B. 8 MB
C. 2 MB
D. 24 MB

parsed.stem: Consider a machine with 64 MB physical memory and a 32-bit virtual address space. If the page size is 4 KB, what is the approximate size of the page table?

sourceQuestion:
In a system with 32 bit virtual addresses and 1 KB page size, use of one-level page tables for virtual to physical address translation is not practical because of
A. the large amount of internal fragmentation
B. the large amount of external fragmentation
C. the large memory overhead in maintaining page tables
D. the large computation overhead in the translation process
sourceAnswer: C. the large memory overhead in maintaining page tables

parsed.stem: In a system with 32 bit virtual addresses and 1 KB page size, use of one-level page tables for virtual to physical address translation is not practical because of

options.text: the large amount of internal fragmentation
the large amount of external fragmentation
the large memory overhead in maintaining page tables
the large computation overhead in the translation process

sourceQuestion:
A processor uses 2-level page tables for virtual to physical address translation. Page tables for both levels are stored in the main memory. Virtual and physical addresses are both 32 bits wide. The memory is byte addressable. For virtual to physical address translation, the 10 most significant bits of the virtual address are used as index into the first level page table while the next 10 bits are used as index into the second level page table. The 12 least significant bits of the virtual address are used as offset within the page. Assume that the page table entries in both levels of page tables are 4 bytes wide. Further, the processor has a translation look-aside buffer (TLB), with a hit rate of 90%. The TLB caches recently used virtual page numbers and the corresponding physical page numbers. The processor also has a physically addressed cache with a hit rate of 90%. Main memory access time is 10 ns, cache access time is 1 ns, TLB access time is also 1 ns.

Assuming that no page faults occur, the average time taken to access a virtual address is approximately (to the nearest 0.5 ns):
A. 1.5 ns
B. 2 ns
C. 3 ns
D. 4 ns

A. 8 KB
B. 12 KB
C. 16 KB
D. 20 KB

sourceQuestion:
A CPU generates 32-bit virtual addresses. The page size is 4 KB. The processor has a translation lookaside buffer (TLB) which can hold a total of 128 page table entries and is 4-way set associative. The minimum size of the TLB tag is:
A. 11 bits
B. 13 bits
C. 15 bits
D. 20 bits

parsed.stem: A CPU generates 32-bit virtual addresses. The page size is 4 KB. The processor has a translation lookaside buffer (TLB) which can hold a total of 128 page table entries and is 4-way set associative. The minimum size of the TLB tag is:

sourceQuestion:
A computer system supports 32-bit virtual addresses as well as 32-bit physical addresses. Since the virtual address space is of the same size as the physical address space, the operating system designers decide to get rid of the virtual memory entirely. Which one of the following is true?
A. Efficient implementation of multi-user support is no longer possible
B. The processor cache organization can be made more efficient now
C. Hardware support for memory management is no longer needed
D. CPU scheduling can be made more efficient now
sourceAnswer: A. Efficient implementation of multi-user support is no longer possible

parsed.stem: A computer system supports 32-bit virtual addresses as well as 32-bit physical addresses. Since the virtual address space is of the same size as the physical address space, the operating system designers decide to get rid of the virtual memory entirely. Which one of the following is true?

options.text: Efficient implementation of multi-user support is no longer possible
The processor cache organization can be made more efficient now
Hardware support for memory management is no longer needed
CPU scheduling can be made more efficient now

sourceQuestion:
A processor uses 36-bit physical addresses and 32-bit virtual addresses, with a page frame size of 4 Kbytes. Each page table entry is of size 4 bytes. A three-level page table is used for virtual to physical address translation, where the virtual address is used as follows:
- Bits 30-31 are used to index into the first level page table.
- Bits 21-29 are used to index into the second level page table.
- Bits 12-20 are used to index into the third level page table.
- Bits 0-11 are used as offset within the page.
The number of bits required for addressing the next level page table (or page frame) in the page table entry of the first, second, and third level page tables are respectively:
A. 20, 20, 24
B. 24, 24, 24
C. 24, 24, 20
D. 25, 25, 24

parsed.stem:
A processor uses 36-bit physical addresses and 32-bit virtual addresses, with a page frame size of 4 Kbytes. Each page table entry is of size 4 bytes. A three-level page table is used for virtual to physical address translation, where the virtual address is used as follows:
- Bits 30-31 are used to index into the first level page table.
- Bits 21-29 are used to index into the second level page table.
- Bits 12-20 are used to index into the third level page table.
- Bits 0-11 are used as offset within the page.
The number of bits required for addressing the next level page table (or page frame) in the page table entry of the first, second, and third level page tables are respectively:

sourceQuestion:
The essential content(s) in each entry of a page table is/are:
A. Virtual page number
B. Page frame number
C. Both virtual page number and page frame number
D. Access right information

parsed.stem: The essential content(s) in each entry of a page table is/are:

options.text: Both virtual page number and page frame number

sourceQuestion:
A multilevel page table is preferred in comparison to a single level page table for translating virtual address to physical address because:
A. It reduces the memory access time to read or write a memory location.
B. It helps to reduce the size of page table needed to implement the virtual address space of a process.
C. It is required by the translation lookaside buffer.
D. It helps to reduce the number of page faults in accessing the memory locations.
sourceAnswer: B. It helps to reduce the size of page table needed to implement the virtual address space of a process.

parsed.stem: A multilevel page table is preferred in comparison to a single level page table for translating virtual address to physical address because:

options.text: It reduces the memory access time to read or write a memory location.
It helps to reduce the size of page table needed to implement the virtual address space of a process.
It is required by the translation lookaside buffer.
It helps to reduce the number of page faults in accessing the memory locations.

sourceQuestion:
Let the page fault service time be 10 milliseconds (ms) in a computer with average memory access time being 20 nanoseconds (ns). If one page fault is generated for every 10^6 memory accesses, what is the effective access time for memory?
A. 21 ns
B. 30 ns
C. 23 ns
D. 35 ns

parsed.stem: Let the page fault service time be 10 milliseconds (ms) in a computer with average memory access time being 20 nanoseconds (ns). If one page fault is generated for every 10^6 memory accesses, what is the effective access time for memory?

sourceQuestion:
A computer uses 86-bit virtual address, 32-bit physical address, and a three-level paged page table organization. The page table base register stores the base address of the first-level table (T1), which occupies exactly one page. Each entry of T1 stores the base address of a page of the second-level table (T2). Each entry of T2 stores the base address of a page of the third-level table (T3). Each entry of T3 stores a page table entry (PTE). The PTE is 32 bits in size. The processor used in the computer has a 1 MB 16-way set associative virtually indexed physically tagged cache. The cache block size is 64 bytes.
What is the size of a page in KB in this computer?
A. 2
B. 4
C. 8
D. 16

parsed.stem:
A computer uses 86-bit virtual address, 32-bit physical address, and a three-level paged page table organization. The page table base register stores the base address of the first-level table (T1), which occupies exactly one page. Each entry of T1 stores the base address of a page of the second-level table (T2). Each entry of T2 stores the base address of a page of the third-level table (T3). Each entry of T3 stores a page table entry (PTE). The PTE is 32 bits in size. The processor used in the computer has a 1 MB 16-way set associative virtually indexed physically tagged cache. The cache block size is 64 bytes.
What is the size of a page in KB in this computer?

sourceQuestion:
A computer uses 86-bit virtual address, 32-bit physical address, and a three-level paged page table organization. The page table base register stores the base address of the first-level table (T1), which occupies exactly one page. Each entry of T1 stores the base address of a page of the second-level table (T2). Each entry of T2 stores the base address of a page of the third-level table (T3). Each entry of T3 stores a page table entry (PTE). The PTE is 32 bits in size. What is the minimum number of page colours needed to guarantee that no two synonyms map to the same set in the cache of this computer?
A. 2
B. 4
C. 8
D. 16

sourceQuestion:
Consider a system with byte-addressable memory, 32-bit logical addresses, 4 kilobyte page size, and page table entries of 4 bytes each. The size of the page table of a process in the system in megabytes is _________.
A. 1
B. 4
C. 8
D. 16

parsed.stem: Consider a system with byte-addressable memory, 32-bit logical addresses, 4 kilobyte page size, and page table entries of 4 bytes each. The size of the page table of a process in the system in megabytes is _________.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a process executing on an operating system that uses demand paging. The average time for a memory access in the system is 10 units if the corresponding memory page is available in memory, and 10000 units if the memory access causes a page fault. It has been experimentally measured that the average time taken for a memory access in the process is 1000 units.

Which one of the following is the correct expression for the page fault rate experienced by the process?

A. (D - M) / (X - M)
B. (X - M) / (D - X)
C. (D - X) / (X - M)
D. (X - M) / (D - M)

parsed.stem:
Consider a process executing on an operating system that uses demand paging. The average time for a memory access in the system is 10 units if the corresponding memory page is available in memory, and 10000 units if the memory access causes a page fault. It has been experimentally measured that the average time taken for a memory access in the process is 1000 units.

Which one of the following is the correct expression for the page fault rate experienced by the process?

sourceQuestion:
Assume that in a certain computer, the virtual addresses are 64 bits long and the physical addresses are 48 bits long. The memory is word addressable. The page size is 4 kB and the word size is 4 bytes. The Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB) in the address translation path has 128 valid entries. At most how many distinct virtual address spaces can be simultaneously supported by the hardware so that the TLB does not need to be flushed for a context switch?

A. 16 x 2^20
B. 256 x 2^20
C. 4 x 2^20
D. 8 x 2^20

parsed.stem: Assume that in a certain computer, the virtual addresses are 64 bits long and the physical addresses are 48 bits long. The memory is word addressable. The page size is 4 kB and the word size is 4 bytes. The Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB) in the address translation path has 128 valid entries. At most how many distinct virtual address spaces can be simultaneously supported by the hardware so that the TLB does not need to be flushed for a context switch?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a paging system that uses a 1-level page table residing in main memory and a TLB for address translation. Each main memory access takes 10 ns and TLB lookup takes 2 ns. Each page transfer to/from disk takes 5,000,000 ns. Assume that the TLB hit ratio is 0.9 and the page fault rate is 10^-6 (i.e., one in a million accesses causes a page fault). Assume that for 20% of the total page faults, a dirty page has to be written back to disk before the required page is read from disk.

The average time (in ns) for address translation using the above system is ___

A. 13.0
B. 14.6
C. 15.4
D. 13.9

parsed.stem:
Consider a paging system that uses a 1-level page table residing in main memory and a TLB for address translation. Each main memory access takes 10 ns and TLB lookup takes 2 ns. Each page transfer to/from disk takes 5,000,000 ns. Assume that the TLB hit ratio is 0.9 and the page fault rate is 10^-6 (i.e., one in a million accesses causes a page fault). Assume that for 20% of the total page faults, a dirty page has to be written back to disk before the required page is read from disk.

The average time (in ns) for address translation using the above system is ___

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following tasks is/are the responsibility/responsibilities of the memory management unit (MMU) in a system with paging-based memory management?

A. Allocate a new page table for a newly created process
B. Translate a virtual address to a physical address using the page table
C. Raise a trap when a page is tried to be written to but is marked read-only in the page table
D. Raise a trap when a page referenced is not found in memory (page fault)

parsed.stem: Which of the following tasks is/are the responsibility/responsibilities of the memory management unit (MMU) in a system with paging-based memory management?

options.text: Allocate a new page table for a newly created process
Translate a virtual address to a physical address using the page table
Raise a trap when a page is tried to be written to but is marked read-only in the page table
Raise a trap when a page referenced is not found in memory (page fault)

sourceQuestion:
Consider a 32-bit system with page size 1KB and page table entries of size 4 bytes each. Assume 1KB = 2^10 bytes. The OS uses a 2-level page table for memory management, with the page table containing an outer page directory and an inner page table. The OS allocates a page for the outer page directory upon process creation. The OS uses demand paging when allocating memory for the inner page table, i.e., a page of the inner page table is allocated only if it contains a valid page table entry.

An active process in this system accesses 2000 unique pages during its execution, and none of the pages are swapped out to disk. After it completes its execution, let X denote the minimum and Y denote the maximum number of pages across the two levels of the page table of the process.

The value of X + Y is:

A. 10
B. 2009
C. 2010
D. 4010

parsed.stem:
Consider a 32-bit system with page size 1KB and page table entries of size 4 bytes each. Assume 1KB = 2^10 bytes. The OS uses a 2-level page table for memory management, with the page table containing an outer page directory and an inner page table. The OS allocates a page for the outer page directory upon process creation. The OS uses demand paging when allocating memory for the inner page table, i.e., a page of the inner page table is allocated only if it contains a valid page table entry.

An active process in this system accesses 2000 unique pages during its execution, and none of the pages are swapped out to disk. After it completes its execution, let X denote the minimum and Y denote the maximum number of pages across the two levels of the page table of the process.

The value of X + Y is:

sourceQuestion:
In a virtual memory system, size of the virtual address is 32 bits, size of the physical address is 30 bits, page size is 4 Kbytes and size of each page table entry is 32 bits. The main memory is byte addressable. Which one of the following is the maximum number of bits that can be used for storing protection and other information in each page table entry?

A. 2
B. 10
C. 12
D. 14

parsed.stem: In a virtual memory system, size of the virtual address is 32 bits, size of the physical address is 30 bits, page size is 4 Kbytes and size of each page table entry is 32 bits. The main memory is byte addressable. Which one of the following is the maximum number of bits that can be used for storing protection and other information in each page table entry?

sourceQuestion:
A paging scheme uses a Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB). A TLB-access takes 10 ns and the main memory access takes 50 ns. What is the effective access time (in ns) if the TLB hit ratio is 90% and there is no page-fault?
A. 54
B. 60
C. 65
D. 75

parsed.stem: A paging scheme uses a Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB). A TLB-access takes 10 ns and the main memory access takes 50 ns. What is the effective access time (in ns) if the TLB hit ratio is 90% and there is no page-fault?

sourceQuestion:
Match the following flag bits used in the context of virtual memory management on the left side with the different purposes on the right side of the table below.

  Name of the bit | Purpose
I.   Dirty        | a. Page initialization
II.  R/W          | b. Writeback policy
III. Reference    | c. Page protection
IV.  Valid        | d. Page replacement policy

A. I-b, II-a, III-d, IV-c
B. I-b, II-c, III-d, IV-a
C. I-c, II-b, III-a, IV-d
D. I-b, II-c, III-a, IV-d

parsed.stem:
Match the following flag bits used in the context of virtual memory management on the left side with the different purposes on the right side of the table below.

  Name of the bit | Purpose
I.   Dirty        | a. Page initialization
II.  R/W          | b. Writeback policy
III. Reference    | c. Page protection
IV.  Valid        | d. Page replacement policy

sourceQuestion:
In the balanced binary tree in the below figure, how many nodes will become unbalanced when a node is inserted as a child of the node "g"?

The tree structure (AVL balanced):
           a
          / \
         b   c
        / \ / \
       d  e f  g
      /
     h

(All nodes are labeled a through h; 'h' is the left child of 'd'; 'g' is the right child of 'c')

A. 1
B. 3
C. 7
D. 8

In the balanced binary tree in the below figure, how many nodes will become unbalanced when a node is inserted as a child of the node "g"?

The tree structure (AVL balanced):

        / \ / \
       d  e f  g
      /
     h

(All nodes are labeled a through h; 'h' is the left child of 'd'; 'g' is the right child of 'c')

sourceQuestion:
What is the maximum height of any AVL-tree with 7 nodes? Assume that the height of a tree with a single node is 0.

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5

parsed.stem: What is the maximum height of any AVL-tree with 7 nodes? Assume that the height of a tree with a single node is 0.

sourceQuestion:
What is the worst case time complexity of inserting n elements into an AVL-tree with n elements initially?

A. Theta(n log n)
B. Theta(n^2)
C. Theta(n^2 log n)
D. Theta(n^3)

parsed.stem: What is the worst case time complexity of inserting n elements into an AVL-tree with n elements initially?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is TRUE?

A. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)
B. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a complete binary tree is Theta(n log n)
C. The cost of searching a binary search tree is O(log n) but that of an AVL tree is Theta(n)
D. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(n log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)
sourceAnswer: A. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)

parsed.stem: Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)
The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a complete binary tree is Theta(n log n)
The cost of searching a binary search tree is O(log n) but that of an AVL tree is Theta(n)
The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(n log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)

sourceQuestion:
The following Pascal program segment finds the largest number in a two-dimensional integer array A[0..n-1, 0..n-1] using a single loop. Fill up the boxes to complete the program.

Assume that max is a variable to store the largest value and i, j are the indices to the array.

begin
  max := A[0, 0];
  for k := 1 to ___[A] do
    begin
      i := k div n;
      j := k mod n;
      if A[i, j] > max then max := A[i, j]
    end
end

What is the correct expression for box [A]?

A. n^2 - 1
B. n^2
C. n - 1
D. 2n - 1

parsed.stem:
The following Pascal program segment finds the largest number in a two-dimensional integer array A[0..n-1, 0..n-1] using a single loop. Fill up the boxes to complete the program.

Assume that max is a variable to store the largest value and i, j are the indices to the array.

begin
  max := A[0, 0];
  for k := 1 to ___[A] do
    begin
      i := k div n;
      j := k mod n;
      if A[i, j] > max then max := A[i, j]
    end
end

What is the correct expression for box [A]?

sourceQuestion:
In a compact single dimensional array representation for lower triangular matrices (i.e., all elements above the diagonal are zero) of size n x n, non-zero elements (i.e., elements of the lower triangle) of each row are stored one after another, starting from the first row, the index of the (i, j)th element of the lower triangular matrix in this new representation is:

A. i + j - 1
B. i(i-1)/2 + j
C. (j-1) + i
D. i + j^(3/2)

parsed.stem: In a compact single dimensional array representation for lower triangular matrices (i.e., all elements above the diagonal are zero) of size n x n, non-zero elements (i.e., elements of the lower triangle) of each row are stored one after another, starting from the first row, the index of the (i, j)th element of the lower triangular matrix in this new representation is:

sourceQuestion:
An array A contains n >= 1 positive integers in the locations A[0], A[1], ..., A[n-1]. The following program fragment prints the length of a shortest sequence of consecutive elements of A, such that the sum of their values is >= M, a given positive number. It prints 'n+1' if no such sequence exists. Complete the program by filling in the boxes.

begin
  i := 0;
  j := 0;
  sum := A[0];
  min_len := n + 1;
  while j < n do
  begin
    if sum < M then
    begin
      j := j + 1;
      if j < n then sum := sum + A[j]
    end
    else
    begin
      if [ ] < min_len then min_len := [ ];
      sum := sum - A[i];
      i := i + 1
    end
  end;
  writeln(min_len)
end

The two boxes [ ] should be filled with:
A. (j - i + 1) and (j - i + 1)
B. (j - i) and (j - i)
C. (j - i + 1) and (j - i)
D. (j - i) and (j - i + 1)
